How to Write a CV in 2026: Step-by-Step Guide to Land Interviews

March 8, 2026 · 8 min read · By ApliSense Team

Writing a strong CV is one of the most important career skills you can develop. In today's job market, your CV is often the first — and sometimes only — impression a recruiter has of you before deciding whether to invite you for an interview. A poorly written CV can cause even the most qualified candidates to be overlooked. A well-crafted one can open doors that your experience alone might not.

What Is a CV and Why Does It Matter?

A CV (Curriculum Vitae) is a structured document summarizing your professional history, education, skills, and qualifications. Research shows recruiters spend an average of six to eight seconds reading a CV before making an initial decision. Before any interviews, a recruiter or automated system scans your CV to determine whether you meet the basic criteria for the role. This means your document needs to communicate your value clearly and immediately.

How Long Should a CV Be?

For most professionals with fewer than ten years of experience, a one-page CV is the gold standard. If you have more than ten years of relevant experience or are applying for a senior or academic position, two pages is acceptable. Beyond two pages is generally discouraged for most industry roles. The key principle is relevance over volume — every line on your CV should earn its place by demonstrating value to the reader.

Step 1: Personal Details and Contact Information

The top of your CV should include your full name displayed prominently, a professional email address, your phone number with country code if applying internationally, your city and country of residence, a link to your LinkedIn profile, and a portfolio or GitHub link if relevant to your field. Do not include your date of birth, marital status, nationality, or a photograph unless specifically required by the employer or local custom. In the UK and most Western countries, including these details is considered outdated and may inadvertently introduce bias into the hiring process.

Step 2: Professional Summary

Directly below your contact details, include a professional summary — a three to five sentence paragraph that introduces who you are as a professional. A strong professional summary states your professional identity and level of experience, highlights your two or three most relevant skills or achievements, indicates the type of role you are targeting, and uses active, confident language that reflects your professional value.

Example: "Results-driven software engineer with five years of experience building scalable web applications using React, Node.js, and AWS. Proven track record of reducing system load times by 40% and shipping features that improved user retention. Seeking a senior engineering role at a product-focused technology company."

Avoid vague phrases like "hardworking team player" or "passionate about technology." These are empty words that every applicant uses. Be specific and quantify wherever possible.

Step 3: Work Experience

Work experience is typically the most important section of your CV. List your positions in reverse chronological order — most recent first. For each role, include: job title, company name and location, dates of employment, and three to six bullet points describing your key responsibilities and achievements.

The bullet points are where most applicants lose marks. The most common mistake is listing duties rather than achievements. Use the CAR framework for each bullet point: Context, Action, Result. For example: "Grew LinkedIn follower count by 180% in six months through targeted content strategy, increasing qualified leads by 35%." Quantify wherever possible — numbers, percentages, revenue figures, and timeframes make your achievements tangible and memorable.

Step 4: Education

For most professionals, education should follow work experience. For recent graduates or academic applications, education may appear first. Include degree or qualification title, institution name and location, year of graduation, and grade or classification if it is strong. If you have a university degree, you generally do not need to list secondary school qualifications unless specifically requested.

Step 5: Skills

Include a clearly labeled Skills section listing your key technical and professional competencies. This section is critical for ATS optimization — automated systems scan specifically for a list of skills to match against the job description. Organize your skills into categories where possible. Only list skills you can genuinely discuss in an interview — padding a skills section with buzzwords you cannot back up will hurt you at the interview stage.

Step 6: Additional Sections

Depending on your background and the role you are applying for, you may want to include certifications and professional training, language proficiencies with proficiency level indicated, volunteer work relevant for graduates to demonstrate transferable skills, projects useful for developers and designers to showcase portfolio work, or publications and research for academic CVs and senior professional roles.

CV Formatting Best Practices

Use a clean, professional font such as Calibri, Arial, or Georgia at 10 to 12pt for body text. Use consistent heading styles and keep margins between 1.5cm and 2.5cm on all sides. Avoid tables, text boxes, images, headers, and footers — these elements confuse many ATS systems. Use standard section headings that ATS systems recognize: Work Experience, Education, Skills, Certifications. Save and submit your CV as a PDF unless the employer specifically requests a Word document. Name your file professionally — for example "JohnSmith_CV_2026.pdf".

Tailoring Your CV for Each Application

One of the most impactful things you can do for your job search is to tailor your CV to each role you apply for. Read the job description carefully and identify the key skills, qualifications, and responsibilities it emphasizes. Then ensure those terms appear naturally in your skills section, work experience, and professional summary. This process both improves your ATS pass rate and makes your CV more compelling to the human recruiter who reads it afterward. Using a tool like ApliSense to analyze your CV against specific job roles can accelerate this process significantly.

Before You Submit

Proofread for spelling, grammar, and punctuation errors. Check that all dates are accurate and that there are no unexplained gaps. Confirm that contact details are correct and up-to-date. Verify that the file name is professional. Run an ATS check using ApliSense to verify keyword alignment before submitting — this takes only a few minutes and can significantly improve your results.
